items.mistake: Forgetting to shuffle data between epochs
items.fix: Shuffle training indices each epoch (e.g. `DataLoader(..., shuffle=True)`) so consecutive batches are not biased by file or class order.
items.why: Ordered data makes mini-batches correlate strongly; gradients point in a skewed direction and training slows or fails to generalize.
items.example: If all class-A images come first, early batches only see A; the model shifts weights for A until B appears, causing loss spikes.

items.mistake: Not calling zero_grad before backward on each step
items.fix: Call `optimizer.zero_grad()` (or `zero_grad(set_to_none=True)`) before every `loss.backward()` so gradients accumulate only within the current batch.
items.why: PyTorch accumulates gradients by default; stale gradients from prior batches corrupt the update.
items.example: After two steps without zeroing, `param.grad` is g₁ + g₂ but you intended only g₂—effective learning rate doubles unintentionally.

items.mistake: Treating mini-batch loss as the full training loss
items.fix: Log running averages of batch loss for monitoring; evaluate full validation loss on a held-out set at epoch boundaries.
items.why: A single batch loss is a noisy snapshot; decisions like early stopping need validation metrics, not one jagged batch value.
items.example: Batch loss jumps from 0.4 to 0.9 on one hard batch—that is normal noise, not necessarily a reason to reload a checkpoint.
